sections.paragraph: dual. the loins, the lower part of the back (so called from the idea of strength; see the root מָתַן ), Greek ὀσφύς, which was bound round with the girdle, 2Ki 4:29 9:1 Isa 20:2 Jer 1:17 Gen 37:34, on which burdens were sustained, Psa 66:11 the seat of pain in parturient women, Isa 21:3 Nah 2:11 also the seat of strength (see the orig.; comp. Latin elumbis, delumbare for debilitate); hence מָחַץ מָתְנֵי פ׳ “to crush any one’s loins,” i.e. to crush him wholly, Deu 33:11 compare Eze 21:11 tottering loins, used of a man nearly falling, Psa 69:24. Wherein it differs from the word יָרֵךְ thigh, see under that word.-(Arab. مَتْنَانِ and Syr. ܡܰܬܢܳܬܳܐ id. more rarely there is also used the sing. مَتْنُ one side of the loins or lower back, protuberant with flesh and nerves).
